title: Aureolaria dispersa
intro: Aureolaria dispersa, also known as the rosy puccoon or rosy twining pink, is a perennial flowering plant in the borage family (Boraginaceae). The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its health benefits, including its ability to aid in digestion, reduce inflammation, and promote the removal of mucus from the body. Bioactive constituents of Aureolaria dispersa include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made from this plant include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat respiratory issues and colds.
taxonomy: Aureolaria dispersa is a species of flowering plant in the family Orobanchaceae. It is a hemiparasitic plant, meaning it obtains some of its nutrients by parasitizing the roots of other plants. Aureolaria dispersa is native to North America, where it is commonly found in dry to moist environments, including prairies and open woods. The plant is known for its yellow flowers, which are often clustered in whorls along the stem. As a member of the Orobanchaceae family, Aureolaria dispersa is closely related to other hemiparasitic plants, such as Indian pipe and lousewort.
